attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of mouth and jaw pain at the site of a dental extraction performed three months ago. She has a medical history of ischaemic heart disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and osteoporosis. Her medications include aspirin, tiotropium, fluticasone, and alendronate which she has taken for five years. She does not smoke and drinks minimal alcohol.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 19/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.6°C. There is a 10 mm area of exposed bone in the left anterior mandible without discharge or bleeding. There is no cervical lymphadenopathy. Cranial nerve examination is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ): exposed bone at a dental extraction site in a patient taking bisphosphonates. Medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw is defined as exposed bone or bone that can be probed through an intraoral or extraoral fistula in the maxillofacial region that has persisted for more than eight weeks in a patient with current or previous treatment with anti-resorptive or anti-angiogenic agents and no history of radiation therapy.

Osteomyelitis of the jaw typically presents with more systemic features including fever, severe pain, and purulent discharge. While it can occur following dental procedures, the lack of inflammatory signs and the history of bisphosphonate use make MRONJ more likely.

Dental pulp infection causes acute, severe pain localised to the affected tooth. It would not cause exposed bone and typically occurs in the setting of dental caries or trauma rather than following a dental extraction.

Trigeminal neuralgia causes paroxysmal, severe, electric shock-like pain in the distribution of one or more branches of the trigeminal nerve. It would not cause exposed bone in the jaw.

Oral cancer typically presents as a non-healing ulcer or mass. While it can cause bone destruction, the temporal relationship to dental extraction and use of bisphosphonates makes MRONJ more likely.

The management of MRONJ involves optimising oral hygiene, antimicrobial mouth rinses, and pain control. More severe cases may require surgical debridement. Prevention strategies include dental assessment before starting bisphosphonates and maintaining good oral hygiene. While MRONJ is a serious complication, it is important to note that the absolute risk is low (1-10%) and should be weighed against the benefits of preventing osteoporotic fractures, which carry significant morbidity and mortality.
learningPoints: Medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ) should be suspected in patients on anti-resorptive medications who develop exposed jaw bone, particularly after dental procedures.
Risk factors for MRONJ include duration of anti-resorptive therapy, recent dental procedures, and poor oral hygiene.
Prevention of MRONJ includes dental assessment before starting anti-resorptive therapy and maintaining good oral hygiene.
The risk of MRONJ must be balanced against the benefits of preventing osteoporotic fractures, which carry significant morbidity and mortality.
references: eTG complete [Internet]. Melbourne (Vic): Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd; 2019. Medication related osteonecrosis of the jaw: dental considerations [updated 2019 Dec; cited 2021 Feb 6]. Available from: https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au/viewTopic?topicfile=medication-related-osteonecrosis-jaws&guidelineName=Oral%20and%20Dental&topicNavigation=navigateTopic#toc_d1e47
Kanwar N, Bakr MM, Meer M, et al. Emerging therapies with potential risks of medicine- related osteonecrosis of the jaw: a review of the literature. Br Dent J. 2020; 228:886-92.
